perturbation.original_text: EACH PARTY HERETO WAIVES, TO THE FULLEST EXTENT PERMITTED BY APPLICABLE LAW, ANY RIGHT IT MAY HAVE TO A TRIAL BY JURY IN RESPECT OF ANY DISPUTE ARISING FROM OR UNDER THIS AGREEMENT.
perturbation.changed_text: EACH PARTY HERETO WAIVES, TO THE FULLEST EXTENT PERMITTED BY APPLICABLE LAW, ANY RIGHT IT MAY HAVE TO A TRIAL BY JURY IN RESPECT OF ANY DISPUTE ARISING FROM OR UNDER THIS AGREEMENT. Shipper shall release, indemnify, defend, and hold harmless Transporter and its affiliates, directors, officers, employees, agents, consultants, representatives, and invitees from and against all claims and losses arising out of or relating to (i) the operations of Shipper, (ii) any breach of this agreement by Shipper, except to the extent attributable to the negligence, willful misconduct or fault of Transporter, and (iii) operations or activities upstream or downstream of the Gathering System, except to the extent attributable to the negligence, willful misconduct or fault of Transporter.
perturbation.explanation: By embedding the indemnification clause within the jury waiver, its prominence is reduced, potentially hindering understanding and acceptance. Indemnification clauses typically merit their own section for clarity and emphasis.
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas law emphasizes clear and understandable contracts, particularly regarding significant risk transfers like indemnification. Burying the indemnification clause reduces its clarity and potentially violates this principle by not presenting it in a clear and conspicuous manner, increasing the likelihood that it will be overlooked or misunderstood.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ CIVIL PRACTICE AND REMEDIES CODE CHAPTER 154. ALTERNATIVE DISPUTE RESOLUTION PROCEDURES CIVIL PRACTICE AND REMEDIES CODE TITLE 7. ALTERNATE METHODS OF DISPUTE RESOLUTION CHAPTER 154. ALTERNATIVE DISPUTE RESOLUTION PROCEDURES SUBCHAPTER A. GENERAL PROVISIONS The following section was amended by the 89th Legislature. Pending publication of the current statutes, see H.B. 40 , 89th Legislature, Regular Session, for amendments affecting the following section. Sec. 154.001. DEFINITIONS. In this chapter: (1) "Court" includes an appellate court, district court, constitutional county court, statutory county court, family law court, probate court, municipal court, or justice of the peace court. (2) "Dispute resolution organization" means a private profit or nonprofit corporation, political subdivision, or public corporation, or a combination of these, that offers alternative dispute resolution services to the public. Added by Acts 1987, 70th Leg., ch. 1121, Sec. 1, eff. June 20, 1987. Sec. 154.002. POLICY. It is the policy of this state to encourage the peaceable resolution of disputes, with special consideration given to disputes involving the parent-child relationship, including the mediation of issues involving conservatorship, possession, and support of children, and the early settlement of pending litigation through voluntary settlement procedures. Added by Acts 1987, 70th Leg., ch. 1121, Sec. 1, eff. June 20, 1987. Sec. 154.003. RESPONSIBILITY OF COURTS AND COURT ADMINISTRATORS. It is the responsibility of all trial and appellate courts and their court administrators to carry out the policy under Section 154.002 . Added by Acts 1987, 70th Leg., ch. 1121, Sec. 1, eff. June 20, 1987. SUBCHAPTER B. ALTERNATIVE DISPUTE RESOLUTION PROCEDURES Sec. 154.021. REFERRAL OF PENDING DISPUTES FOR ALTERNATIVE DISPUTE RESOLUTION PROCEDURE. (a) A court may, on its own motion or the motion of a party, refer a pending dispute for resolution by an alternative dispute resolution procedure including: (1) an alternative dispute resolution system established under Chapter 26 , Acts of the 68th Legislature, Regular Session, 1983 (Article 2372aa, Vernon's Texas Civil Statutes); (2) a dispute resolution organization; or (3) a nonjudicial and informally conducted forum for the voluntary settlement of citizens' disputes through the intervention of an impartial third party, including those alternative dispute resolution procedures described under this subchapter. (b) The court shall confer with the parties in the determination of the most appropriate alternative dispute resolution procedure. (c) Except as provided by agreement of the parties, a court may not order mediation in an action that is subject to the Federal Arbitration Act (9 U.S.C. Sections 1-16). Added by Acts 1987, 70th Leg., ch. 1121, Sec. 1, eff. June 20, 1987. Amended by: Acts 2009, 81st Leg., R.S., Ch. 621 (H.B. 1083 ), Sec. 1, eff. June 19, 2009. Sec. 154.022. NOTIFICATION AND OBJECTION. (a) If a court determines
perturbation.justification: The change creates a contradiction by embedding a significant indemnification clause within a jury waiver, diminishing its clarity. The Law Explanation states that Texas law requires clear contracts, especially for risk transfers like indemnification. Embedding the indemnification clause reduces its clarity and could violate this principle, increasing the risk of it being overlooked. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses alternative dispute resolutions but does not address the specific requirement for clear and conspicuous risk transfer clauses, however, combined with the Law Explanation, the structural flaw introduces a legal contradiction as described in the Perturbation Definition by potentially hindering understanding and acceptance.

perturbation.original_text: All payments are due within 10 days of receipt of the invoice by ACH or wire transfer, unless the Transporter determines in a manner not unreasonably discriminatory that the financial condition of Shipper or Shipper's guarantor (if any) is or has become impaired or unsatisfactory or Transporter determines in a manner not unreasonably discriminatory it necessary to do so, in which case the payment due date shall be that specified in a written notice to the Shipper.
perturbation.changed_text: All payments are due within 10 days of receipt of the invoice by ACH or wire transfer, unless the Transporter determines in a manner not unreasonably discriminatory that the financial condition of Shipper or Shipper's guarantor (if any) is or has become impaired or unsatisfactory or Transporter determines in a manner not unreasonably discriminatory it necessary to do so, in which case the payment due date shall be that specified in a written notice to the Shipper. Shipper shall release, indemnify, defend, and hold harmless Transporter and its affiliates, directors, officers, employees, agents, consultants, representatives, and invitees from and against all claims and losses arising out of or relating to (i) the operations of Shipper, (ii) any breach of this agreement by Shipper, except to the extent attributable to the negligence, willful misconduct or fault of Transporter, and (iii) operations or activities upstream or downstream of the Gathering System, except to the extent attributable to the negligence, willful misconduct or fault of Transporter.
perturbation.explanation: By appending the indemnification clause to the payment terms, it becomes less visible and might be overlooked. Indemnification clauses are typically in separate, prominent sections. Adding it here creates confusion between payment deadlines and liabilities for operational issues.
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas law requires contracts to be clear and easily understandable, especially regarding liability shifts like indemnification. This change hides the indemnification within a paragraph concerning payment terms, potentially making it less noticeable and understandable, and not presented in a clear and conspicuous manner, violating the spirit of transparent contracting as promoted by Texas legal principles.

perturbation.justification: The added indemnification clause, embedded within the payment terms, creates a structural flaw by obscuring a significant liability shift. The Law Explanation states that Texas law favors clear and easily understandable contracts, particularly concerning liability shifts. The placement of the indemnification clause within the payment terms makes it less noticeable, potentially violating the spirit of transparent contracting. This aligns with the 'Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction' definition, where a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant section, making it difficult to interpret. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses alternative dispute resolution, it doesn't directly address the clarity or placement of contract terms. The issue is the obscured placement of the indemnification clause.

perturbation.original_text: EXCEPT WITH REGARD TO OBLIGATIONS TO INDEMNIFY A PARTY FOR CLAIMS MADE BY THIRD PARTIES, NEITHER PARTY SHALL BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY (OR ITS AFFILIATES) PURSUANT TO THIS AGREEMENT FOR ANY CONSEQUENTIAL, INCIDENTAL, INDIRECT, OR SPECIAL DAMAGES OR LOSSES OR ANY PUNITIVE, EXEMPLARY, TREBLE, OR SIMILAR DAMAGES ARISING OUT OF OR RELATING TO THIS AGREEMENT OR THE PERFORMANCE OF, OR FAILURE TO PERFORM, ITS OBLIGATIONS HEREUNDER, EVEN IF SUCH DAMAGES OR LOSSES ARE CAUSED BY THE SOLE, JOINT, OR CONCURRENT NEGLIGENCE, STRICT LIABILITY, OR OTHER FAULT OF THE PARTY WHOSE LIABILITY IS BEING WAIVED HEREBY.
perturbation.changed_text: EXCEPT WITH REGARD TO OBLIGATIONS TO INDEMNIFY A PARTY FOR CLAIMS MADE BY THIRD PARTIES, NEITHER PARTY SHALL BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY (OR ITS AFFILIATES) PURSUANT TO THIS AGREEMENT FOR ANY CONSEQUENTIAL, INCIDENTAL, INDIRECT, OR SPECIAL DAMAGES OR LOSSES OR ANY PUNITIVE, EXEMPLARY, TREBLE, OR SIMILAR DAMAGES ARISING OUT OF OR RELATING TO THIS AGREEMENT OR THE PERFORMANCE OF, OR FAILURE TO PERFORM, ITS OBLIGATIONS HEREUNDER, EVEN IF SUCH DAMAGES OR LOSSES ARE CAUSED BY THE SOLE, JOINT, OR CONCURRENT NEGLIGENCE, STRICT LIABILITY, OR OTHER FAULT OF THE PARTY WHOSE LIABILITY IS BEING WAIVED HEREBY. Shipper shall furnish, or cause the producer from which Crude Petroleum is purchased to furnish, install, calibrate, and maintain continuous pressure recording devices at or near injection points to monitor pipeline operating pressures. Copies of the recording charts taken from these recording devices shall be furnished to Transporter on a weekly basis by Shipper. Shipper shall also furnish Transporter copies of annual calibration certificates for recording devices. Transporter reserves the right to witness calibration of these devices, and Shipper shall notify Transporter at least 48 hours prior to the initiation of such calibration procedures.
perturbation.explanation: Appending the section related to monitoring pipeline operating pressures, which includes obligations for the Shipper to install and maintain equipment, to the Limitation of Liability clause makes these obligations less prominent and potentially overlooked. This is especially problematic since those requirements have safety implications.
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas law favors clear contract language and structure, especially when dealing with safety-related requirements. This perturbation reduces the visibility of the Shipper's monitoring obligations by placing it at the end of the Limitation of Liability clause, which is not related to the obligation to monitor the pressure.

NOTIFICATION AND OBJECTION. (a) If a court determines
perturbation.justification: The Perturbation Explanation describes that placing the Shipper's monitoring obligations within the Limitation of Liability clause makes them less prominent and potentially overlooked, especially since those requirements have safety implications. The Law Explanation states that Texas law favors clear contract language and structure, especially when dealing with safety-related requirements, and this change reduces the visibility of the Shipper's monitoring obligations. The original contract focuses on limiting liability, and the addition of the new clause about pressure recording devices introduces an unrelated subject. The added requirement should likely be in a separate section focused on operational requirements or safety protocols, rather than being buried within a clause designed to limit financial liability. This aligns with the definition of a Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction, which says that a legal contradiction arises when a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section, making it difficult for parties to locate or interpret. Scraped Law Snippet 1, while not directly on point, emphasizes the importance of alternative dispute resolution and the responsibilities of courts, suggesting a broader context where clarity and enforceability of contracts are valued. The altered text undermines the visibility and prominence of these safety-related obligations.
